name: Sureshvara
dates_notes: Direct disciple of Śaṅkara; identification with pre-conversion Maṇḍana is disputed (Hiriyanna negative; traditional sectarian sources affirmative).

lineage_polemical.note: Sureśvara's jñāna-only / Brahman-as-locus-of-avidyā position is structurally opposed to Maṇḍana's bhāvanā / jīva-as-locus-of-avidyā stream.

core_thesis: Sureśvara, Śaṅkara's direct disciple, holds three theses that together define his stream of Advaita. First, on the saving cognition: *brahman*-knowledge arises as *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti* from *mahāvākya*-cognition (paradigmatically *tat tvam asi*) and is itself directly liberating; no supplementary *prasaṅkhyāna* or repeated meditative *bhāvanā* is required. [*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.98–99](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.98-99) states this directly: because *tat-tvam-asi-ādi-vākyāni* reveal a *svatas-siddhārtha* (a self-established reality), no *arthāntara* can be projected into them, and *mokṣa* arises *jñānād eva* — from knowledge alone. This is precisely where Sureśvara opposes Maṇḍana. Second, on the locus of *avidyā*: ignorance is one, not plural, and is located in *brahman*, not the *jīva* ([*Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Vārttika* 1.4.402–411](cite://sureshvara/brhadaranyaka-varttika/1.4.402-411), *Sambandha-Vārttika* portion). The reason is structural: the *jīva* is itself a product of *avidyā* and cannot without circularity be its substrate ([*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 3.2–4](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/3.2-4)). Third, on the relation of *karma* and *jñāna*: the two stand as *bādhya* and *bādhaka* (sublated and sublator) and cannot occupy one locus together ([*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.55–56](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.55-56)). *Karma* presupposes the agent–object structure that *jñāna* dissolves; any *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* path to *mokṣa* is therefore incoherent. The argument is sustained against Maṇḍana's *Brahma-Siddhi* and against the Bhartṛprapañca stream ([*Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Vārttika* 1.4](cite://sureshvara/brhadaranyaka-varttika/1.4), on Yājñavalkya-Maitreyī dialogue). The pure *naiṣkarmya* state of *jñāna* is *mokṣa* itself ([*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 4.69–76](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/4.69-76)). This stream — *brahman*-locus *avidyā*, *vākya*-cognition as immediately sufficient, strict *jñāna*-only — is the template that Padmapāda's lineage and Sarvajñātman will systematize as *Vivaraṇa*.

engaged_works.title: Naishkarmya-Siddhi
engaged_works.summary: Independent *prakaraṇa* in four chapters, mixed verse and prose, defending the *naiṣkarmya* (action-less) reading of liberation against the *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* alternative. Chapter 1 establishes *brahman*-knowledge as the sole means of *mokṣa* and refutes the view that Vedic action — even at its most refined — contributes to liberation. The structural argument at [NS 1.55–56](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.55-56) turns on the relation of *bādhya* and *bādhaka*: knowledge and action exclude one another like sun and night and cannot occupy one locus together. Chapter 2 analyzes the *mahāvākya tat tvam asi*, the *jahad-ajahad-lakṣaṇā* (joint inclusive-exclusive implication) by which the *vākya* is read non-dually, and the *adhyāsa*-mechanism. [NS 2.9–10](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.9-10) makes explicit that *anvaya-vyatireka* (concomitance-and-exclusion) reasoning is required for true grasp of sentence-meaning — a reply to the charge that *jñāna*-only Advaita is anti-interpretive quietism. Chapter 3 takes up specific *pūrvapakṣa* objections; Maṇḍana's *prasaṅkhyāna-vāda* is the principal target. Chapter 4 treats *jīvanmukti* and the residual conditions of the liberated knower. Securely authored; the systematic doctrinal complement to Sureśvara's *Vārttikas*. Critical edition: Hino, with R. Balasubramanian's translation.

engaged_works.title: Brhadaranyaka-Varttika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored vārttika on Śaṅkara's Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya.
engaged_works.summary: Massive *vārttika* in verse — over eleven thousand stanzas — on Śaṅkara's *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad-Bhāṣya*, organized by *adhyāya* and *brāhmaṇa* following the underlying Upaniṣadic and *bhāṣya* structure. The *Sambandha-Vārttika*, glossing the *bhāṣya*'s introductory portion, is doctrinally pivotal. Sureśvara uses it to develop, at length, three positions for which there is *bhāṣya* warrant but which receive their first systematic exposition here: the relation of *karma* to *jñāna*, the impossibility of *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*, and the *brahman*-locus of *avidyā*. The *vārttika* is the principal vehicle for the *Vivaraṇa*-stream's elaboration of Śaṅkara's positions and is the most cited *vārttika* in later Advaita literature. It also preserves substantial fragments of Bhartṛprapañca via the *pūrvapakṣa* portions, making it a primary source for the modern reconstruction of that lost commentary. Securely authored; the standard Ānandāśrama edition is the working text.

engaged_works.title: Taittiriya-Varttika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored vārttika on Śaṅkara's Taittirīya-Bhāṣya.
engaged_works.summary: Verse *vārttika* on Śaṅkara's *Taittirīya-Upaniṣad-Bhāṣya*, organized by the three *vallīs* of the underlying Upaniṣad. The doctrinally fertile portion is the treatment of *Brahmānanda-Vallī*. Sureśvara develops three points there at length. First, the analysis of *satyaṃ jñānam anantaṃ brahma* (Taittirīya 2.1.1) as *svarūpa-lakṣaṇa* — definition by essential nature — rather than as the predication of independent *guṇas* of *brahman*. Second, the defense of Śaṅkara's reading of *ānanda-maya-kośa* as still belonging to the *upādhi*-order rather than as identical with *brahman*; this is precisely the point Rāmānuja will reject. Third, the elaboration of the relation between the *kośa*-hierarchy, the *avasthā-traya*, and *adhyāsa*. The work is shorter and tighter than the *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Vārttika* and is treated by later Advaita as the standard *vārttika* on the *pañca-kośa* analysis. Securely authored; the standard Ānandāśrama edition is the working text. With the *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Vārttika*, this work constitutes Sureśvara's *vārttika* corpus on Śaṅkara's Upaniṣad-bhāṣyas.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
ātmānātmā ca loke 'smin
pratyakṣādi-pramāṇataḥ
siddhau tayor anātmā tu
sarvatraivātma-pūrvakaḥ
key_passages.text_critical_note: The archive OCR underlying this run reads `siddhas`; the dual nominative `siddhau` is adopted as the grammatically required reading.

pada_analysis.gloss: from means of knowledge beginning with perception
preceded by the Self / dependent on the Self

samasa_vigrahas.note: Ablative expression: by means of the pramāṇas beginning with perception.
naña-tatpuruṣa: the not-Self, defined privatively against the Self.
What is objectified never appears apart from prior Self-presence.

karaka_structure.note: First member of the dual predication with siddhau.
Second member of the dual predication with siddhau.
Genitive delimiting the latter claim about the two.

key_passages.english_close: In this world, the Self and the not-Self [stand thus]. By means of pramāṇas such as perception. They are both established. But, of those two — in such establishment — the not-Self is everywhere preceded by the Self.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Objectivity never shows up on its own: whatever counts as not-Self appears only in dependence on the Self.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: what is not the Self, the object-side of experience

key_passages.why_this_passage: This verse is a fair programmatic statement of Sureśvara's ontology and epistemology: the not-Self is never independently given, but is always disclosed within prior Self-presence. Because the local Sanskrit extraction was most reliable for the Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi in this run, the selection leans on his systematic treatise rather than the more weakly OCR'd vārttika PDFs.
key_passages.source_edition: A. J. Alston (trans.), The Realization of the Absolute: The “Naiṣkarmya Siddhi” of Śrī Sureśvara, Shanti Sadan, London, 1959; transcribed from materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/suresvara_naiskarmya_siddhi_alston_1959_archive.pdf and checked against the matching archive.org OCR derivative.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
anvaya-vyatirekābhyāṃ vinā
vākyārtha-bodhanaṃ na syāt
tena vinājñāna-prahāṇaṃ
nopapadyate
vinājñāna-prahāṇena
puruṣārthaḥ su-durlabhaḥ
tasmād yathokta-siddhy-arthaṃ
paro grantho 'vatāryate

pada_analysis.morphology: abl sg neut (surface sandhi of tasmāt before y-)

pada_analysis.gloss: for the sake of accomplishing what has just been stated

samasa_vigrahas.note: The paired reasoning of concomitance and exclusion.
Cognition of sentence-meaning, not mere word-recognition.
Surface sandhi after vinā; the object removed is ignorance, not knowledge.
The final human good, here mokṣa.
For achieving what has just been taught.

verb_modality.note: The next section is brought in / introduced.

key_passages.english_close: Without anvaya-vyatireka [reasoning by positive concomitance and exclusion]. There would be no understanding of the sentence-meaning. Without that [understanding]. The removal of ignorance is not possible. Without the removal of ignorance. The human end is very difficult to attain. Therefore, in order to accomplish what has just been stated. A further section [of the treatise] is now introduced.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Sureśvara insists that hearing the great sentence is not a brute event: disciplined anvaya-vyatireka inquiry prepares genuine grasp of what the sentence says, and only that grasp removes ignorance.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: the meaning of a full sentence
ignorance, the root misapprehension to be removed

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is a fair representative of Sureśvara's epistemology because it makes explicit that mahāvākya-cognition requires disciplined understanding of sentence-meaning and is ordered to the destruction of ignorance. It also shows why his jñāna-only position is not anti-interpretive quietism: reasoning serves realization by clearing the way for śruti's disclosure.
key_passages.source_edition: A. J. Alston (trans.), The Realization of the Absolute: The “Naiṣkarmya Siddhi” of Śrī Sureśvara, Shanti Sadan, London, 1959; transcribed from materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/suresvara_naiskarmya_siddhi_alston_1959_archive.pdf and checked against the matching archive.org OCR derivative.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
bādhya-bādhaka-bhāvāc ca
pañcāsyoraṇayor iva
eka-deśānavasthānāt
na samuccayatā tayoḥ
ayathā-vastv-avidyā syād
vidyā tasyā virodhinī
samuccayas tayor evaṃ
ravi-śarvarayor iva
key_passages.text_critical_note: The archive OCR reads `eka-desanavasthanan`; `eka-deśānavasthānāt` is adopted as the syntactically and philosophically coherent reading ('because the two do not abide in one place together').

pada_analysis.morphology: abl sg masc (surface sandhi of bhāvāt before ca)
pada_analysis.gloss: because of the relation of what is sublated and what sublates
of a lion and a sheep
because of not remaining in one place

pada_analysis.morphology: 3sg vidhi-liṅ P (surface sandhi of syāt before v-)

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: bādhyaś ca bādhakaś ca tayor bhāvāt
samasa_vigrahas.note: Knowledge and action stand as sublator and sublated, not as collaborators.
The two do not remain together in one locus.
Avidyā is mis-taking reality, not a true cognition alongside knowledge.
Sun and night exclude one another just as knowledge and ignorance do.
Genitive dual of the pair 'lion and sheep' — used as the example of mutually-exclusive co-presence.

karaka_structure.note: Ablative of reason explaining why samuccayatā is denied.

verb_modality.note: Gnomic optative used in philosophical definition.

key_passages.english_close: Because they stand in the relation of what is sublated and what sublates. Just as in the case of a lion and a sheep [together]. And because they do not remain in one place together. Therefore, there is no conjunction of the two. Ignorance would be misapprehension of reality (ayathā-vastv-avidyā). Knowledge is its opponent (virodhin). Their conjunction would [therefore] be thus. Just like the conjunction of the sun and night — that is, impossible.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Sureśvara's point is structural, not rhetorical: action belongs to the sphere sustained by ignorance, while liberating knowledge cancels that very sphere, so a joint saving economy is incoherent.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: a supposed conjunction or joint operation

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is one of Sureśvara's most position-defining formulations of the impossibility of jñāna-karma-samuccaya, a thesis central to his dispute with ritualist and post-Maṇḍana alternatives. It is not a decorative analogy passage: the kāraka logic of mutual exclusion is exactly how he blocks any theory that makes action co-salvific with knowledge.
key_passages.source_edition: A. J. Alston (trans.), The Realization of the Absolute: The “Naiṣkarmya Siddhi” of Śrī Sureśvara, Shanti Sadan, London, 1959; transcribed from materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/suresvara_naiskarmya_siddhi_alston_1959_archive.pdf and checked against the matching archive.org OCR derivative.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
tat-tvam-asy-ādi-vākyānāṃ
svatas-siddhārtha-bodhanāt
arthāntaraṃ na saṃdraṣṭuṃ
śakyate tridaśair api
ataḥ sarvāśramāṇāṃ tu
vāk-manaḥ-kāya-karmabhiḥ
svānuṣṭhitair na muktiḥ syāj
jñānād eva hi sā yataḥ

pada_analysis.gloss: of sentences beginning with 'tat tvam asi'
because of their revealing a self-established reality
by acts of speech, mind, and body

pada_analysis.morphology: abl sg neut (surface sandhi of jñānāt before eva)

samasa_vigrahas.note: The class of great identity-statements beginning with 'tat tvam asi'.
The sentences reveal what is already self-established, not a future action.

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: vācā manasā kāyena ca kriyamāṇaiḥ karmabhiḥ
samasa_vigrahas.note: The full triad of enacted discipline.
A meaning other [than the one the mahāvākya self-establishes].

karaka_structure.note: Reason for denying any imposed secondary meaning.
Agent/instrument in the passive possibility construction.
Genitive delimiting who is under discussion.
Instrumental of purported means to mokṣa that is denied.
Ablative of source/cause: liberation comes from knowledge alone.

key_passages.english_close: Statements beginning with 'tat tvam asi' reveal a self-established meaning. Therefore, another meaning. Cannot be seen [projected] into them. Not even by the gods. Therefore, for the members of all āśramas. With acts of speech, mind, and body. Even when those acts are self-performed, liberation does not arise. For it [arises] only from knowledge — because it proceeds from that alone.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Sureśvara treats the mahāvākya as declarative revelation, not as an encoded command to do something else. Once that is granted, karma cannot be promoted from preparatory discipline to the direct producer of mokṣa.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: the great Upaniṣadic sentence 'That thou art'

key_passages.why_this_passage: This pair of verses gives the cleanest concise statement of Sureśvara's jñāna-only doctrine: mahāvākya sentences state reality, and because they do so, they cannot be subordinated to ritual or meditative action. It is representative rather than cherry-picked because the whole first book is ordered toward exactly this conclusion, and these verses explicitly summarize that argument.
key_passages.source_edition: A. J. Alston (trans.), The Realization of the Absolute: The “Naiṣkarmya Siddhi” of Śrī Sureśvara, Shanti Sadan, London, 1959; transcribed from materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/suresvara_naiskarmya_siddhi_alston_1959_archive.pdf and checked against the matching archive.org OCR derivative.

quoted_by.what_quoted: Sureśvara's *brahman*-locus *avidyā* and *jñāna*-only theses are routinely deployed in the *Advaita-Siddhi* against Vyāsatīrtha's *Nyāyāmṛta*.
Sureśvara's positions are foundational reference for the Vivaraṇa-school doxography.
Sarvajñātman explicitly succeeds Sureśvara's stream and systematizes his *brahman*-locus *avidyā* doctrine.
